speaker: Robert Akerlof
title: Getting the Picture
abstract: Standard economic theory assumes that people have perfect information processing ability: they can derive all logical consequences of information immediately. In reality, however, people struggle to work out the consequences of information. In this paper, we offer a theory of the reasoning process—how people go about “connecting the dots.” In our theory, an agent may have all of the information needed to draw a conclusion yet they still fail to see it. Our key assumption is that agents have limited “working memory.” This constrains the number of pieces of information—“dots”— an agent can think about and connect at once. We explore how agents analyze pieces of information and work their way to a big picture—or, “narrative”—and how narratives, in turn, shape the agent’s view of the parts. We show that limited working memory makes sense of why people struggle with tradeoffs, suffer from choice overload, are influenced by defaults, engage in satisficing, selectively attend to attributes, are subject to primacy effects, and may vacillate between interpretations. We apply the framework to political persuasion, showing how supplying a simple narrative can lock in an interpretation of ambiguous evidence

speaker: Murali Agastya
title: Musings on Mediation with Maximum Entropy (and a Little Noise)
abstract:
A mediator/policy maker begins with what she thinks is the fair outcome  (correlated strategy) in a strategic form game. Of course, to implement   this,  it should at least be a  correlated equilibrium (CE), but what if it   is not? I argue that incorporating CE incentive constraints can be viewed as  a problem of information updating.  A small set of axioms then leads to a simple updating rule: select the correlated equilibrium that is closest to the fair outcome in relative entropy.
Treating equilibrium selection as an inference procedure is novel. If one adds noisy communication from the mediator to rationally inattentive players,  the selected equilibrium  is closely related to the Nash Bargaining Solution.

speaker: Egor Stark
title: Strategic Attribute Learning
abstract: A researcher allocates a budget of informative tests across multiple unknown attributes to influence a decision-maker. We derive the researcher's equilibrium learning strategy by solving an auxiliary single-player problem. The attribute weights in this problem depend on how much the researcher and the decision-maker disagree. If the researcher expects an excessive response to new information, she forgoes learning altogether. In an organizational context, we show that a manager favors more diverse analysts as the hierarchical distance grows. In another application, we show how an appropriately opposed advisor can constrain a discriminatory politician, and identify the welfare-inequality Pareto frontier of researchers.
